title: Cat Bleeding from Ear Canal: Causes and Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Severe ear bleeding in cats is a high-priority emergency. Understand the causes, urgency, and why you should contact a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bleeding from the ear canal is a high-urgency emergency that requires immediate veterinary care to prevent permanent hearing loss or neurological complications. Taking a photo of the ear can help the clinic prepare for your arrival.

What does it mean if my pet is bleeding from their ear canal?

Bleeding from the ear canal, known medically as otorrhagia, occurs when blood originates from the internal structures of the ear rather than a simple scratch on the outer ear flap. This condition often signals damage to the middle or inner ear, a ruptured eardrum, or severe inflammation within the canal itself.

Is bleeding from my pet's ear canal considered an emergency?

  • Bleeding from the ear canal is a High Urgency emergency because the ear is located in close proximity to the brain and contains sensitive neurological and vascular structures.
  • Active bleeding can indicate significant trauma, deep-seated infections, or systemic clotting disorders.
  • Immediate veterinary intervention is required to prevent permanent hearing loss or further complications.

How does taking a photo of my pet's ear help the veterinary team?

  • A photo allows the vet to see the volume and color of the blood and detect the presence of pus or debris.
  • It helps the medical team assess the level of swelling before the area is cleaned.
  • This information helps the clinic prepare necessary diagnostic tools, such as an otoscope or sedation, before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with bleeding from the ear canal, potential causes include ear canal tumors (more common in middle-aged to older cats) and nasopharyngeal polyps (more common in young cats). Clinical signs of ear canal tumors include unilateral chronic otic discharge that may be ceruminous, purulent, mucoid, or hemorrhagic, as well as necrotic odor, head shaking, and ear scratching. Nasopharyngeal polyps, benign inflammatory growths, can also cause purulent discharge if associated with chronic bacterial otitis media. Trauma can also cause bleeding and rapid hematoma development. Diagnostic evaluation should include deep otoscopic examination, possibly requiring flushing and suctioning of purulent discharge for visualization; radiographs, CT, or MRI of the bullae may be helpful. Neurologic signs such as deafness, vestibular signs (head tilt, ataxia, nystagmus), facial nerve palsy, or Horner syndrome may indicate middle or inner ear involvement.

Chapter: Oncology, Otolaryngology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 534)

faqs.question: Should I apply ear drops or cleaners to stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: No. You should never put liquids or medications into a bleeding ear canal. If the eardrum is ruptured, these substances can enter the middle ear and cause severe neurological damage or deafness.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of ear canal bleeding?
faqs.answer: Common causes include severe ear mite infestations, nasopharyngeal polyps, ear canal tumors, foreign objects (like grass seeds), or blunt force trauma to the head.

faqs.question: How can I safely transport my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a dark, quiet carrier to reduce stress. Do not attempt to bandage the head tightly, as this can cause distress; instead, place a soft towel in the carrier to absorb any blood during the trip.
